justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap explicitly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker — 'No distributed tracing at all. This is the single biggest observability gap.' (2) 30-second log delay called out as incident response concern — 'on-call sees stale data during fast-moving incidents.' (3) Stale runbook flagged — '18-month-old runbook is a liability, not an asset.' (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified as the #1 gap — 'CRITICAL — there is no recovery path.' (5) Multiple specific recommendations provided: recovery mechanism, distributed tracing, runbook update, plus additional items like stuck-order gauge, error categorization, and latency alerting. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: The 3am incident walkthrough table is exceptional — it makes the gaps visceral and concrete rather than abstract, showing exactly how each missing capability compounds into a prolonged incident
Goes well beyond the rubric by identifying additional gaps: no stuck-order gauge metric, no error categorization in metrics, no latency alerting, no circuit breakers, no dependency health metrics
Correctly notes alert threshold is adequate (PagerDuty routing works) while also identifying a subtle gap: static threshold cannot distinguish burst vs sustained degradation just below threshold
Recovery tooling gap is not just flagged but deeply analyzed — identifies that without outbox/event log, forced transitions via SQL won't notify downstream services, leading to a second incident
Recommendations are prioritized in a practical order (recovery mechanism first, tracing later) rather than listing everything as equally urgent
Appropriate escalation routing — recognizes stuck-order recovery is an architecture decision (routes to MJ) and runbook staleness is a process issue (routes to Coach K)
